data.title: Bank of America CEO warns interest-bearing stablecoins could pull $6T from US banks
data.description:
Bank of America CEO Brian Moynihan warned that interest-bearing stablecoins could pull as much as $6 trillion out of the US banking system, arguing that large-scale deposit migration would reduce lending capacity and push borrowing costs higher.

The comments surfaced after a crypto investor shared a screenshot from Bank of America’s earnings call transcript on X.

During the call, Moynihan pointed to Treasury-cited studies showing that a significant share of bank deposits could shift into stablecoins if issuers are allowed to pay interest. He said such products would function more like “a money market mutual fund concept,” with funds held in cash, central bank reserves or short-term Treasurys rather than deployed for lending.

Moynihan said such a shift would move deposits off bank balance sheets, shrinking credit availability, particularly for small and mid-sized businesses that rely more heavily on bank loans than capital markets.

The comments come amid stalled progress in crypto legislations in the United States. On Wednesday, the US Senate Banking Committee postponed a markup of the crypto market structure bill that had been scheduled for Thursday, with committee Chair Tim Scott saying the delay was needed to allow for further bipartisan negotiations. Scott did not provide a new date for the markup.

The postponement followed a similar move by the Senate Agriculture Committee, which earlier this week pushed its own markup of the crypto bill to Jan. 27.

Related: Coinbase could pull CLARITY Act support over stablecoin rewards ban

The debate over stablecoin yield

Whether stablecoin issuers or the exchanges and third parties that distribute their tokens should be allowed to offer yield has emerged as a key point of contention in negotiations across Congress.

Banking groups have said yield-bearing stablecoin products function similarly to unregulated investment products and have been vocal about closing any loopholes that allows yield to be passed to tokenholders.

On Jan. 7, the Community Bankers Council wrote a letter to lawmakers that echoed concerns raised by Moynihan, warning that as much as $6.6 trillion in bank deposits could be at risk if restrictions are not enforced. They wrote:

If billions are displaced from community bank lending, small businesses, farmers, students, and home buyers in towns like ours will suffer. Crypto exchanges and the constellation of stablecoin-affiliated companies are not designed to fill the lending gap, nor will they be able to offer FDIC-insured products.

Crypto industry leaders are divided on the current state of the CLARITY Act, a bill aimed at clarifying the regulatory framework for digital assets that has passed in the House of Representatives and is awaiting Senate consideration.

On Wednesday, Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong said the company could not support the Senate Banking Committee’s draft of the bill because, among other things, it would “draft amendments that would kill rewards on stablecoins, allowing banks to ban their competition.”

He added that Coinbase would “rather have no bill than a bad bill” if the legislation advances in its current form.

Other industry leaders have taken a more optimistic view. On Thursday, a16z Crypto managing partner Chris Dixon said that while the bill is “not perfect” and still requires changes, advancing the CLARITY Act is necessary if the US wants to remain a leading hub for crypto innovation.

data.title: Mostly dry January could hit Argentina's corn output, meteorologist says
data.description:
Limited and scattered rainfall in Argentina's Pampas region for most of January could weigh on the country's 2025/26 corn crop, following several weeks with little to no precipitation, a meteorologist said on Thursday.

German Heinzenknecht said since December, there had been little rainfall throughout the core agricultural region and Buenos Aires province.

"Now, after 15 days of very light and scattered rain in January, things are getting quite tight," said Heinzenknecht, a specialist at the Applied Climatology Consulting firm.

Argentina, the world's third-largest corn exporter, has been forecast by its main grain exchanges to produce a record harvest this season.

The December-February period is the warmest and most moisture-demanding time in Argentina, which is currently in its Southern Hemisphere summer.

In this context, Heinzenknecht said rainfall in the necessary amounts might not arrive until the end of January.

"These are negative signs for crops, especially early-planted corn, which is currently flowering. It will likely reduce its yield potential," he added.

According to Wednesday's weekly weather report from the Buenos Aires Grains Exchange, Argentina's core agricultural region is expected to receive between 10 and 25 mm (0.4 to 1 inch) of rainfall over the next seven days.

On Wednesday, the Rosario Grains Exchange, which expects a record corn harvest of 62 million tons, also highlighted the adverse weather recorded in important corn-growing areas of Argentina during the last month, following 2025's above-average rainfall.

"It's an unfortunate turn in the excellent rainfall patterns we've been seeing, and it's presenting some difficulties, especially for early-planted corn," the Rosario Grains Exchange said in its monthly crop report.

SOYBEANS ALSO AT RISK

In its weekly crop status report on Thursday, the Buenos Aires Grains Exchange said soybean crops are also at risk due to the limited forecast rainfall.

According to the exchange, producers have planted 93.9% of the 17.6 million hectares forecast for soybeans this 2025/26 harvest, and 91.7% of the 7.8 million hectares dedicated to corn in the 2025/26 cycle.

The Rosario Grains Exchange estimated Argentina's soybean production at 47 million tons.

data.title: It's Hard to Manage Relationships With Divorcing Couples. How These Financial Advisors Do It. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Cheryl Winokur Munk

Sevasti Balafas managed money for friends who went through a messy seven-year divorce. Once the divorce was final, she dropped the husband as a client. "There were a lot of things I saw behind the scenes on how he was treating her, and I didn't want to be in that situation," says Balafas, the 46-year-old chief executive of GoalVest Advisory, based in New York City. "I didn't need that emotional burden."

Advisors have different practices when it comes to retaining clients after a divorce. Some advisors erect hard-and-fast barriers, but many make these decisions on a case-by-case basis. The CFP Board has a new guide to ethically handling divorcing clients to help in these situations, but often it comes down to an advisor's comfort level and circumstances.

Here are six ways next-gen advisors navigate complications that may arise when clients split:

Retain a divorcing couple, with caveats. Many advisors develop close relationships with clients, and because of this, both parties may want to remain clients after a divorce, says Zach Teutsch, the 42-year-old managing partner of Values Added Financial in Washington, D.C. He is happy to work with both, with certain caveats. For example, Teutsch says his firm generally won't maintain an association if the divorcing spouses have an adversarial relationship.

It's understandable that clients see their financial advisor as a comforting constant in their lives at a time of upheaval, says Austin Warner, a 36-year-old senior wealth planner in the Raleigh, N.C., office of Radnor, Pa.-based Callan Family Office. "Having that trusted resource, where you've had that longstanding relationship, is someone you can lean into."

Consider making a clean break. Some advisors warn against maintaining relationships with divorced spouses. Chris Hernandez, the 42-year-old financial advisor and a founding partner at Strategic Capital in Austin, Texas, will help divorcing clients separate their assets as amicably as possible, according to the terms of the divorce. He'll also help ensure that new accounts are opened and the funds are split appropriately.

But after the divorce, he recommends that one of them find another advisor. Often, the spouse that he's closest to remains with the firm. Hernandez makes this suggestion to avoid potential conflicts of interest or negative feelings related to the divorce that could affect the advisory relationship. "You can't avoid what you're feeling, so starting a new relationship might be the best for the client," Hernandez says.

Set boundaries. When advisors retain both relationships, they have to be careful not to cross lines by sharing private financial or personal information, unless there's a specific reason to do so and both clients agree. This is similar to the rules advisors face when working with siblings, or parents and adult children. "If you're not listed on the portfolio, I don't talk to you about finances," Balafas says.

Sometimes, however, divorced clients have mutual interests, such as saving for their children's college education. If they have a good relationship, it can be helpful to have joint discussions, if they are amenable, Teutsch says. Advisors should ask their compliance professionals whether there are questions about how to handle a particular situation, he adds.

Marissa Beyer, a 43-year-old senior wealth advisor at Fidato Wealth in Middleburg Heights, Ohio, says the firm requires clients to notify it immediately if there is a change in household status. If there's a distribution from a joint account, both clients are notified. Fidato Wealth implemented these policies after an incident about two years ago. The firm was unaware that clients were getting divorced, so it didn't raise red flags when the wife withdrew $50,000 from the couple's joint account. Advisors later discovered that the couple was no longer living together and that the withdrawal was initiated without the husband's knowledge or approval.

The firm also has a rule to prevent divorced couples from working with the same lead advisor. "It's sort of like a nice Chinese Wall," Beyer says. "We're just trying to make sure that there's no conflict of interest."

Know when to bow out. A few years ago, Teutsch of Values Added Financial worked with a divorcing couple in which one spouse made an allegation of abuse and obtained a court order of protection. The other spouse claimed the accuser had a mental illness. The firm sent each spouse a letter saying that it enjoyed the relationship but couldn't continue to represent either of them. They generated a list of about seven potential advisors outside the firm with different approaches and specialties and provided their contact information. They gave the same list to both clients.

Be careful to stay in your lane. Sometimes as part of the divorce process, clients need specific expertise, such as a mediator or a Certified Divorce Financial Analyst. That doesn't mean they have to give up a longstanding relationship with their advisor, but they might want to bring an expert in for certain purposes, such as tax planning. "I think that's often a good idea," Teutsch says.

Get comfortable managing challenges. Advisors should know that if they continue to work with divorced couples, certain conflicts could arise. Client events are one example. Should the advisor invite one or both? What if the exes don't know that each is a client, or what if only one of them knows that information?

This came up recently with a divorced couple that Balafas works with. One of the clients happened to be out of town on the date of the client event, and realizing this, she invited both. She doesn't know how she'd handle the situation in the future, but it will be a judgment call. "We just need to navigate and decide what to do at that time based on the circumstances."

Write to advisor.editors@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: IMF's Georgieva says central bank independence critical, backs Powell
data.description:
International Monetary Fund chief Kristalina Georgieva on Thursday underscored the importance of keeping central banks independent and threw her support behind beleaguered Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, who is facing a Trump administration investigation for renovation cost overruns.

Georgieva told Reuters in an interview that there was ample evidence that central bank independence worked in the interest of businesses and households, and that evidence-based, data-based decision-making is good for the economy.

The IMF managing director said she had worked with Powell and respected his professionalism.

"I have worked with Jay Powell. He is a very good professional, very decent man, and I think that his standing among his colleagues tells the story," she said, when asked about a letter of support signed by her predecessor, Christine Lagarde, now head of the European Central Bank, and other large central banks.

Powell on Sunday disclosed that the Trump administration had opened an investigation into him over cost overruns for a $2.5 billion project to renovate two historical buildings at the Fed's Washington headquarters complex.

Powell denies wrongdoing and has called the unprecedented actions a pretext to put pressure on him for not bowing to Trump's long-running demands for sharply lower interest rates.

The probe has sparked widespread criticism from some key members of Trump's Republican Party in the U.S. Senate, which must confirm his nominee to succeed Powell, along with foreign economic officials, investors and former U.S. government officials from both political parties.

Trump has repeatedly derided Powell's leadership of the Fed and attacked him, often personally, over what he sees as the Fed chair's slow moves to cut interest rates. On Wednesday, he dismissed concerns that eroding central bank independence would undermine the value of the U.S. dollar and spark inflation, telling Reuters, "I don't care."

Georgieva said the IMF looked carefully at issues such as monetary and financial stability, as well as the strength of a country's institutions. It was specifically interested in the Fed, given the role of the U.S. dollar as a reserve currency.

"It would be very good to see that there is a recognition ... that the Fed is precious for the Americans. It is very important for the rest of the world," she said.

Trump has also attempted to fire another Fed official, Governor Lisa Cook, who has challenged her termination in a legal case that will be argued before the Supreme Court next week.

data.title: New York cocoa hits lowest in almost two years on weak Europe grind
data.description:
New York cocoa futures on ICE fell to the lowest level in nearly two years on Thursday as lower-than-expected European grind data reinforced concerns about weak demand.

COCOA

* New York cocoa ​settled down $124, or 2.4%, to $4,966 a metric ton after slumping to $4,839, its lowest in almost two years.

* Europe's fourth-quarter cocoa grind fell 8.3% from a year earlier to 304,470 tons, the European Cocoa Association said on Thursday. Dealers said that a much smaller decline, of about 2%, had been expected.

* A surge in prices in 2024 continues to depress demand for cocoa, with manufacturers reformulating some products to reduce the cocoa content. The European grind was a 21-year-low for the fourth quarter.

* Fourth-quarter grind data for North America is due later on Thursday, while figures for Asia are expected on Friday.

* London cocoa lost 2.3% to 3,650 pounds per ton.

COFFEE

* Robusta coffee settled up $48, or 1.2%, at $4,003 a ton.

* Dealers said farmers in top robusta producer Vietnam appeared reluctant to sell at current prices.

* "While exporters are pushing for lower prices during the peak harvest, farmers are holding onto their beans, hoping for better rates and clearer signals from the international market," said one trader in Vietnam's coffee belt.

* Arabica coffee rose 0.6% to $3.581 per lb.

* Guatemala's coffee exports for the 2024/25 harvest tumbled by nearly 9% from the previous cycle, the country's National Coffee Association said on Wednesday, citing the impact of tariffs imposed last year by U.S. President Donald Trump.

SUGAR

* Raw sugar ​settled down 0.11 cents, or 0.7%, at 14.57 cents per lb.

* Dealers said weak energy prices added to downward pressure on sugar.

* Cane is used to produce either sugar or biofuel ethanol, which means low energy prices can boost production of the sweetener.

* Germany's sugar refiner Nordzucker warned on Thursday it would swing to an annual operating loss on depressed EU sugar prices that are hitting producers across the market.

* White sugar dropped 0.6% to $418.20 a ton.

data.title: Sao Paulo Governor Freitas denies intention to run for presidency this year
data.description:
Sao Paulo state Governor Tarcisio de Freitas on Thursday said he has no plans to run for president this year, restating his support for Brazilian Senator Flavio Bolsonaro's candidacy.

  • Freitas told reporters he has never intended to run in the October election and remains focused on his gubernatorial reelection bid instead.

  • The governor answered questions about a video posted on his social media accounts on Tuesday that was widely seen as an attempt to position himself as a presidential hopeful.

  • In the video, he stated that Brazil needs "a new CEO" and criticized President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva's Workers Party.

  • "I've already said that (Flavio Bolsonaro) is my candidate, and he'll have our support," Freitas said, calling his remarks from earlier in the week an "outburst."

FLAVIO COMMITTED TO RUN

  • Earlier on Thursday, Senator Bolsonaro, who has the backing of his father, imprisoned former President Jair Bolsonaro, said there was no turning back on his candidacy.

  • Some investors had bet on the elder Bolsonaro supporting a more market-friendly name with executive experience, such as the Sao Paulo governor, to challenge Lula.

  • A Genial/Quaest poll released on Wednesday showed Lula with a 45% to 38% lead over Flavio Bolsonaro in a potential run-off.

  • Lula also led Freitas 44% to 39% in the same poll.

data.title: Soybean Futures on Strong Crush Data — Daily Grain Highlights
data.description:
By Kirk Maltais

  • Soybeans for March delivery rose 1% to $10.52 1/2 a bushel on the Chicago Board of Trade Thursday, in reaction to NOPA's monthly report showing an uptick in soybeans crushed for oil and meal.
  • Corn for March delivery fell 0.4% to $4.20 1/2 a bushel.
  • Wheat for March delivery fell 0.5% to $5.10 1/2 a bushel.

HIGHLIGHTS

Robust Report: The release of the National Oilseed Processors Association's latest monthly crush report boosted the soy complex this afternoon, particularly for soyoil futures. NOPA said that the rate of soybeans crushed is up to nearly 225 million bushels, a rise of about 9 million bushels. Yields and production also rose, although stocks did as well. Soybean oil futures also shrugged off the weakness seen in crude oil Thursday, with the two often trading sympathetically due to soybean oil being used to produce biofuel.

Slight Reduction: A reduction in the Brazilian soybean production outlook by Conab on Thursday supported soybeans in morning trade. The Brazilian crop agency now projects the nation's crop at 176.1 million metric tons, down from the 178 million tons Conab had previously projected. That was also the same amount forecasted by the USDA in its WASDE report published Monday. Conab attributed the small reduction to "irregular rainfall" in growing areas, although that rainfall appears to have stabilized and become more consistent.

INSIGHT

Leading the Way: U.S. soybean export sales exceeded analyst expectations this week, due largely to strong sales to China. The USDA said Thursday that soybean export sales for the week ended Jan. 8 totaled 2.07 million metric tons for the 2025/26 and 2026/27 marketing years. Driving this were sales of 1.2 million tons of soybeans to China, with other leading buyers including Egypt, Mexico and Algeria. Analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal this week had forecast soybean export sales to land between 800,000 and 1.8 million tons.

Cheaper Shipping: The cost to ship grains over water is expected to ease in 2026, said the USDA in its weekly Grain Transportation report. That's because the supply of vessels is expected to rise this year, the USDA said. Volatility stemming from warzones globally is also expected to ease, allow ships to travel freely and make supply chains easier to maintain. "With fewer attacks on ships in the Red Sea in recent months, shipping may return to the Suez Canal and Red Sea," said the USDA. "Such a return would raise fleet efficiency and put downward pressure on rates."

Back to Basics: The active speculation seen in farm acre values appears to be cooling off, according to Farmers National Company in a research note. The firm says buyers of farmland are turning their focus back toward fundamental concerns--essentially the ability of someone working the land to turn a profit on their crops. "Areas with high crop yields, diversified farms, and dependable groundwater continue to attract buyers and maintain steady values," said the Omaha-based firm. "Regions facing commodity price pressure, lower yields, or limited alternative income sources are seeing lower demand."

AHEAD

  • The CFTC will release its weekly Commitment of Traders report at 3:30 p.m. ET Monday.
  • The USDA and CME will be closed in observance of Martin Luther King Jr. Day on Monday, with both reopening on Tuesday.
  • The USDA will release its weekly Grain Export Inspections report at 11 a.m. ET Tuesday.

Write to Kirk Maltais at kirk.maltais@wsj.com

data.title: U.S. Natural Gas Futures Settle Mixed — Market Talk
data.description: Natural gas futures put in a mixed session with the Nymex front month edging up after a bearish inventory report and later contracts ending lower. The EIA said natural gas inventories fell by 71 Bcf last week to 3,185 Bcf, which increased the surplus over the five-year average to 106 Bcf from 31 Bcf the week before. "A recent shift in temperature patterns means that today's report could be the lightest withdrawal for at least the next several weeks," Andy Huenefeld of Pinebrook Energy Advisors says in a note. "Inventories appear to be on track to finish the winter above 2.0 Tcf, but that outcome remains subject to realized weather for the balance of the season." Natural gas for February delivery settles up 0.3% at $3.128/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: ICE Review: Canola Lifted by Rising Vegetable Oils
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--Canola futures on the Intercontinental Exchange were on the rise on Thursday, strengthened by vegetable oils.

Reuters reported on Thursday that the United States will finalize its 2026 biofuel quota in March and drop import penalties.

Malaysian palm oil was slightly lower, but Chicago soyoil and European rapeseed were higher.

Crude oil lost nearly US$3 per barrel after U.S. President Donald Trump indicated he would hold off on attacking Iran.

Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney will meet with Chinese President Xi Jinping on Friday. Earlier today, Canadian Foreign Affairs Minister Anita Anand said progress was made during discussions with China to lower tariffs on Canadian products.

At mid-afternoon, the Canadian dollar was down one-tenth of a U.S. cent compared to Wednesday's close.

There were 82,930 canola contracts traded on Thursday, compared to Wednesday when 60,035 contracts changed hands. Spreading accounted for 44,628 of the contracts traded.

Settlement prices are in Canadian dollars per metric tonne.

data.title: Cloudastructure 2025 Year in Review
data.description:
Cloudastructure, Inc., a leader in AI-powered video surveillance and remote monitoring, has released its 2025 Year in Review, showcasing a year marked by significant enterprise adoption and impressive financial growth. The company reported a remarkable 270% year-over-year revenue growth, driven by strong demand for its AI-driven security platform.

Financial Highlights

Cloudastructure achieved approximately $5.0 million in recognized revenue in 2025, a substantial increase from $1.4 million in 2024. The total contract value reached $6.3 million. The fourth quarter alone saw a 306% year-over-year growth, marking the highest quarterly revenue in the company's history.

Business and Operational Highlights

The company's cloud-native architecture integrates AI-powered video surveillance, real-time monitoring, security analytics, and remote guarding into a single operational system. This model has proven effective, with adoption by six of the top ten NMHC-ranked property management firms and approximately 74% customer growth. Key performance metrics for 2025 included a 98% crime deterrence rate, over 11.2 million alerts reviewed, and more than 112,000 live verbal interventions conducted.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

Cloudastructure introduced several innovations in 2025, such as powered enclosure technology for rapid deployments, mobile surveillance trailer solutions, proprietary video processing technology, and renewable-powered data center initiatives. These advancements have enhanced the platform's scalability and operational efficiency.

Management's Perspective

James McCormick, CEO of Cloudastructure, highlighted the company's strong performance and growing demand for its unified, AI-driven security platform. He emphasized the platform's ability to deliver real-time threat deterrence, cost reduction, and actionable security intelligence, positioning Cloudastructure for sustained growth.

Future Outlook

As Cloudastructure enters 2026, the company aims to continue its growth trajectory by expanding its AI-driven security and risk management platform across various industries. With strong customer validation and a scalable platform foundation, Cloudastructure is well-positioned to drive increasing operational impact for its customers.

Original SEC Filing: CLOUDASTRUCTURE, INC. [ CSAI ] - 8-K - Jan. 15, 2026

Disclaimer
This is an AI-powered summary. It may contain inaccuracies. Consider verifying important information with the source. Please note this summary is solely based on documents filed with the SEC.

data.title: Taiwan to Invest $250 Billion in U.S. in New Trade Deal — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Tae Kim

The Commerce Department on Thursday announced the U.S. government and Taiwan have reached a new trade agreement.

The deal "will drive a massive reshoring of America's semiconductor sector. This unprecedented commitment will strengthen U.S. economic resilience, create high-paying jobs, and bolster national security," the Commerce Department said.

The deal stipulates that Taiwan companies will make investments of at least $250 billion to build and expand advanced semiconductor, energy, and artificial intelligence production capacity in the U.S. The Taiwanese government will also provide credit guarantees of at least $250 billion to help the companies make investments.

As part of the agreement, the reciprocal tariff rate on Taiwanese imported goods will be lowered to 15% from 20% and Section 232 duties on Taiwanese auto parts, timber, lumber, and wood derivative products will also be set at 15%.

Generic pharmaceuticals, aircraft components, and certain natural resources will not face any tariffs.

The agreement also stated that future Section 232 duties will be applied to Taiwan semiconductors but the companies will receive credits according to a formula to avoid paying duties if they build new chip making capacity in the U.S. Taiwanese companies may import up to 2.5 times the chips of future planned U.S. capacity without paying during a construction period. Companies that completed projects will be able to import 1.5 times their new U.S. production capacity without paying duties.

TSMC shares, the leading advanced chip manufacturer, which is building fabs in the U.S., did not move much after the announcement. The stock was up about 6% following strong earnings results released earlier in the day.

Write to Tae Kim at tae.kim@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Taiwan Pledges $250 Billion in U.S. Spending in Exchange for Lower Tariffs — WSJ
data.description:
By Amrith Ramkumar

The U.S. and Taiwan signed a trade deal Thursday aimed at boosting American production of semiconductors in exchange for lower tariffs, adding to the Trump administration's efforts to bring critical industries to the U.S.

Under the agreement, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing will add several new factories to its cluster in Arizona as part of a $250 billion investment in the U.S., the Commerce Department said. In exchange, the U.S. is cutting tariffs on Taiwanese goods to 15%, from 20%, and exempting Taiwanese chip companies like TSMC that are investing more in America. The contours of the deal were reported earlier in the week.

The agreement adds to deals with trading partners including the European Union and Japan and a truce with China. The Trump administration is attempting to de-escalate tensions over tariffs to avoid denting the economy while securing added investment in the U.S.

The Taiwan deal focuses on semiconductors because of the dominance of TSMC, which essentially has a monopoly on making the advanced chips that power the modern economy. They are critical for customers from Nvidia to Apple that want to build data centers for training AI models or sell consumer products.

TSMC has received several billion dollars in subsidies through the 2022 Chips and Science Act and will have roughly a dozen plants in Arizona after its latest expansion, including some advanced manufacturing capabilities that give it a sizable footprint outside Taiwan.

Write to Amrith Ramkumar at amrith.ramkumar@wsj.com

data.title: Clarification regarding Intellego Technologies' police report against its former CEO and founder
data.description:
As previously announced, Intellego Technologies AB (“Intellego”) has filed a police report against its former CEO and founder Claes Lindahl.

The police report is primarily based on circumstances which have come to light in the company’s own investigations since November 18, 2025, and in connection with KPMG's forensic investigation into Intellego. KPMG’s investigation is incomplete at this point and no final conclusions have yet been reached. The totality of the information which underlies the police report nevertheless raises suspicions that the Board of Intellego feels are sufficiently serious as to require their reporting to the authorities in the form of a police report. However, our suspicions are not conclusive. The police authorities must review and investigate, KPMG must complete their investigation and reach their conclusions. We have reported our suspicions based on the circumstances we have seen so far.

And, based on what we have seen so far, we believe it is fair to say that most and perhaps all our revenue transactions and cash flow in 2025 are suspect. This does not include our subsidiary Daro in the UK. We have similar concerns about 2024 and some transactions prior to 2024. We have therefore asked KPMG to extend their investigation to go back to 2024 and earlier. This extended scope of investigation will not be reported on January 30 but will come at a later date. The report from KPMG on January 30 will deal with transactions in 2025 only.

With all that in mind, we know that our financial accounts must be restated for 2025 and very probably 2024 and perhaps further back. We have that work underway and our aim is to present our restated accounts with our Q4 report which is now moved up to February 20, so that shareholders will have this information prior to the extraordinary general meeting on February 23.

It is the view of the company’s Board that, until we have all the information that enables us to restate our accounts with a degree of certainty and finality, it would not be appropriate for trading in our shares to resume on NASDAQ First North.

Our focus is on completing the various investigations that are now underway, on facilitating KPMG’s report on January 30, and on restating our accounts for February 20. In parallel, we are also focused on the company’s business going forward. We are speaking with all our stakeholders to protect, secure and preserve our business and assets to ensure our ability to continue the business and ultimately return value to shareholders. We are conducting strategic reviews of all our business areas with a view to preparing and presenting a business case for Intellego’s future. It is primarily this business case that will determine whether trading can resume and whether the company can remain listed on NASDAQ First North. The Board is of the view that if the company is to remain listed and trading is to resume it cannot occur before the end of February.

In summary, Intellego considers that all transactions and cash flow in 2025 can be questioned and is reviewing all of them. Intellego has commissioned KPMG to extend its forensic report to also cover 2024 and suspicious transactions in 2023. Intellego will provide more detailed information once it has received KPMG's final reports, the first of which will be published on January 30.

Intellego is currently unable to assess the specific financial consequences of the suspected crimes referred to in its police report. Intellego is working to restate prior period financial accounts. The company intends to publish its restated accounts by February 20. The company’s total cash on hand as at January 15 amounted to approx. SEK 46 million, excluding funds that are frozen.

Contact

Jacob Laurin, acting CEO and Board Member, Intellego Technologies AB

E-post:jacob.laurin@intellego-technologies.com

About Us

Intellego Technologies develops and manufactures color indicators that show the effect of irradiation with, among other things, invisible ultraviolet light, UV light. Color indicators are used globally in, for example, the disinfection industry, the sunscreen industry, and the manufacturing industry. Intellego's indicators have been developed from the company's patented photochromic ink, which can be adapted to different wavelengths of light and various application areas across a range of different industries. Intellego Technologies was founded in 2011, is headquartered in Stockholm, and is listed on the Nasdaq First North Growth Market.

The company's Certified Adviser on the Nasdaq First North Growth Market Stockholm is Mangold Fondkommission AB.

Intellego's website:https://intellego-technologies.com/en/

Subscribe to Intellego's press releases:https://intellego-technologies.com/sv/pressmeddelanden/

Contact the communications team:communications@intellego-technologies.com

This information is information that Intellego Technologies is obliged to make public pursuant to the EU Market Abuse Regulation. The information was submitted for publication, through the agency of the contact persons set out above, at 2026-01-15 21:15 CET.

Attachments

Clarification regarding Intellego Technologies' police report against its former CEO and founder

data.title: IMF's Georgieva expects to send Ukraine program for board approval in weeks
data.description:
International Monetary Fund chief Kristalina Georgieva on Thursday said she expected to ask the Fund's executive board to approve a new $8.1 billion lending program for Ukraine in a matter of weeks, underscoring the importance of shoring up funding for the war-torn country.

Speaking to Reuters after meetings with Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy and other top Ukrainian officials, Georgieva said much had changed in Ukraine since November, when a preliminary agreement was reached on the program, but the thrust of the program's requirements would remain the same.

"I'm here to see how the country is doing in these unusually harsh times, because I want to make sure that what was agreed in November is implementable as it was agreed," she said. "We recognize that the direction to travel remains the same (but) the way we take these steps, we have to calibrate carefully."

Georgieva said she told the Ukrainian authorities that they had to press forward with removing a VAT exemption for consumer goods that has run into domestic resistance. But she said the IMF would require only that the measure was introduced in parliament, not that it was already passed, before the new program could be approved.

"On the VAT exemptions, we made it very clear that this has to happen. That we cannot possibly have the Ukrainian economy lingering between market economy and non-market economy," she said. "We have to move it forward and the question is not whether. The question is how we do it, how we get the level of support from the parliament."

Georgieva said she explained to Ukrainian officials that this was a must-have requirement. "I was very clear. You know, this, you cannot touch it. You need it for you. You need it for EU accession. You need it to attract the private sector to make the business environment more conducive," she said.

Georgieva said the IMF would assess which measures were easily implemented and which needed to be "calibrated" more carefully. On the VAT exemption, she said the Fund was discussing giving Ukraine a year to drum up support in parliament for passage of the measure, which has proven controversial.
